questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
A line $M$ through $A$ is drawn parallel to $B D$. Point $S$ moves such that its distances from

the line BD and the vertex A are equal. If locus of S cuts M at $\mathrm{T}_2$ and $\mathrm{T}_3$ and AC at $\mathrm{T}_1$, then area of $\Delta T_1 T_2 T_3$ is :
options:
$\frac{1}{2}$ sq. units

$\frac{2}{3}$ sq. units

1 sq. unit

2 sq. units
questions.solution:
$$ \text { Diagonal of square with side length } 2 \text { is } 2 \sqrt{2} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \therefore & & \mathrm{OA} & =\sqrt{2} \\ & & \mathrm{AT}_1 & =\frac{\mathrm{OA}}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Area of } \Delta T_1 T_2 T_3 \end{aligned} $$

                     $$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{AT}_1\right) \times\left(\mathrm{T}_2 \mathrm{~T}_3\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{AT}_1\right) \times 2 \sqrt{2} \\ & =\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{2 \sqrt{2}}=1 \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The locus of a point, which moves such that the sum of squares of its distances from the points (0, 0), (1, 0), (0, 1), (1, 1) is 18 units, is a circle of diameter d. Then d<sup>2</sup> is equal to _____________.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let a circle of radius 4 pass through the origin O , the points $\mathrm{A}(-\sqrt{3} a, 0)$ and $\mathrm{B}(0,-\sqrt{2} b)$, where $a$ and $b$ are real parameters and $a b \neq 0$. Then the locus of the centroid of $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$ is a circle of radius
questions.solution:
Given a circle passes through $O(0,0), A(-\sqrt{3} a, 0)$, and $B(0,-\sqrt{2} b)$. Since $A$ lies on the x -axis and $B$ lies on the y -axis, $\angle A O B=90^{\circ}$. Thus $A B$ is the diameter of the circle.

Diameter $d=2 \times$ radius $=2(4)=8$.

Using distance formula for $A B$ :

$$ \begin{gathered} \sqrt{(-\sqrt{3} a-0)^2+(0-(-\sqrt{2} b))^2}=8 \\ 3 a^2+2 b^2=64 \quad------(1) \end{gathered} $$

Let the centroid of $\triangle O A B$ be $G(h, k)$.

$$ \begin{gathered} h=\frac{0-\sqrt{3} a+0}{3} \Longrightarrow a=\frac{-3 h}{\sqrt{3}}=-\sqrt{3} h \\ k=\frac{0+0-\sqrt{2} b}{3} \Longrightarrow b=\frac{-3 k}{\sqrt{2}} \end{gathered} $$

Substitute $a$ and $b$ into (Eq. 1):

$$ \begin{gathered} 3(-\sqrt{3} h)^2+2\left(\frac{-3 k}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2=64 \\ 3\left(3 h^2\right)+2\left(\frac{9 k^2}{2}\right)=64 \\ 9 h^2+9 k^2=64 \\ h^2+k^2=\frac{64}{9} \end{gathered} $$

The locus is a circle $x^2+y^2=\left(\frac{8}{3}\right)^2$.

Radius $=\frac{8}{3} $

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let a circle C : (x $$-$$ h)2 + (y $$-$$ k)2 = r2, k > 0, touch the x-axis at (1, 0). If the line x + y = 0 intersects the circle C at P and Q such that the length of the chord PQ is 2, then the value of h + k + r is equal to ___________.
questions.solution:
Here, $$O{M^2} = O{P^2} - P{M^2}$$

$${\left( {{{|1 + r|} \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)^2} = {r^2} - 1$$

$$\therefore$$ $${r^2} - 2r - 3 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$r = 3$$

$$\therefore$$ Equation of circle is

$${(x - 1)^2} + {(y - 3)^2} = {3^2}$$

$$\therefore$$ h = 1, k = 3, r = 3

$$\therefore$$ $$h + k + r = 7$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Choose the incorrect statement about the two circles whose equations are given below :

x2 + y2 $$-$$ 10x $$-$$ 10y + 41 = 0 and

x2 + y2 $$-$$ 16x $$-$$ 10y + 80 = 0
options: Distance between two centres is the average of radii of both the circles.
Both circles pass through the centre of each other.
Both circle's centers lie inside region of one another.
questions.solution:
S1 $$ \equiv $$ x2 + y2 $$-$$ 10x $$-$$ 10y + 41 = 0

Centre C1 $$ \equiv $$ (5, 5), radius r1 = 3

S2 $$ \equiv $$ x2 + y2 $$-$$ 16x $$-$$ 10y + 80 = 0

Centre C2 $$ \equiv $$ (8, 5), radius r2 = 3

Distance between centres = 3

Hence both circles pass through the centre of each other, have two intersection point and distance between two centres in average of radii of both the circles.

Hence, option (d) is the incorrect statement.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Points P($$-$$3, 2), Q(9, 10) and R($$\alpha,4$$) lie on a circle C and PR as its diameter. The tangents to C at the points Q and R intersect at the point S. If S lies on the line $$2x-ky=1$$, then k is equal to ____________.
questions.solution:
Now, $\frac{10-2}{9+3} \times \frac{10-4}{9-\alpha}=-1$

$\Rightarrow \frac{8}{12} \cdot 6=\alpha-9 \Rightarrow \alpha=13$

$\therefore 0=(5,3)$ So, $$ m_{O Q}=\frac{7}{4} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} & m_{O R}=\frac{1}{8} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore Q: y-10=\frac{-4}{7}(x-9)$

$\Rightarrow 4 x+7 y=106$

Tangent at $R: y-4=-8(x-13)$

$$ 8 x+y=108\quad...(ii) $$

By (i) and (ii) $S \equiv\left(\frac{25}{2}, 8\right)$, satisfies with the line

$\therefore K=3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: A circle is inscribed in an equilateral triangle of side of length 12. If the area and perimeter of any square inscribed in this circle are $$m$$ and $$n$$, respectively, then $$m+n^2$$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Inradius of $$\triangle A B C=r=\frac{\Delta}{s}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times(12)^2}{18}$$

$$r=2 \sqrt{3}$$

Side length of square is $$a$$, then $$a^2=2 r^2$$

$$\Rightarrow a^2=24$$

Area of square, $$m=24$$

Perimeter of square, $$n=4 \sqrt{24}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow m+n^2=24+384 \\ & =408 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let the set of all values of $r$, for which the circles $(x+1)^2+(y+4)^2=r^2$ and $x^2+y^2-4 x-2 y-4=0$ intersect at two distinct points be the interval $(\alpha, \beta)$. Then $\alpha \beta$ is equal to
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \left|r_1-r_2\right| < c_1 c_2 < r_1+r_2 \\ & r-3 < \sqrt{9+25} < r+3 \\ & r \in(\sqrt{34}-3, \sqrt{34}+3) \\ & \alpha \beta=25 \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let C<sub>1</sub> and C<sub>2</sub> be the centres of the circles x<sup>2</sup>  + y<sup>2</sup> – 2x – 2y – 2 = 0 and x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> – 6x – 6y + 14 = 0 respectively. If P and Q are the points of intersection of these circles, then the area (in sq. units) of the quadrilateral PC<sub>1</sub>QC<sub>2</sub> is :
questions.solution: Area = 2 $$ \times $$ $${1 \over 2}$$.4 = 2

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If the circle $$x^{2}+y^{2}-2 g x+6 y-19 c=0, g, c \in \mathbb{R}$$ passes through the point $$(6,1)$$ and its centre lies on the line $$x-2 c y=8$$, then the length of intercept made by the circle on $$x$$-axis is :

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If the two circles $${(x - 1)^2}\, + \,{(y - 3)^2} = \,{r^2}$$ and $$\,{x^2}\, + \,{y^2} - \,8x\, + \,2y\, + \,\,8\,\, = 0$$ intersect in two distinct point, then :
questions.solution:
$$\left| {{r_1} - {r_2}} \right| < {C_1}{C_2}$$ for intersection

$$ \Rightarrow r - 3 < 5 \Rightarrow r < 8\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)$$

and $${r_1} + {r_2} > {C_1}{C_2},\,$$

$$r + 3 > 5 \Rightarrow r > 2\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)$$

From $$\left( 1 \right)$$ and $$\left( 2 \right),$$ $$2 < r < 8.$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let the centre of the circle $x^2+y^2+2 \mathrm{~g} x+2 f y+25=0$ be in the first quadrant and lie on the line $2 x-y=4$. Let the area of an equilateral triangle inscribed in the circle be $27 \sqrt{3}$. Then the square of the length of the chord of the circle on the line $x=1$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.solution:
For the circle

$$ x^2+y^2+2gx+2fy+25=0, $$

the centre is

$$ (-g,-f) $$

and radius is

$$ r=\sqrt{g^2+f^2-25}. $$

We are given that the centre lies in the first quadrant, so

$$ (-g,-f) $$

is in the first quadrant.

Also, the centre lies on the line

$$ 2x-y=4. $$

So if the centre is $(-g,-f)$, then

$$ 2(-g)-(-f)=4 $$

that is,

$$ -2g+f=4. $$

Now use the information about the equilateral triangle inscribed in the circle.

For an equilateral triangle of side $a$,

$$ \text{Area}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}a^2. $$

Given area is

$$ 27\sqrt{3}. $$

So,

$$ \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}a^2=27\sqrt{3} $$

which gives

$$ a^2=108 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a=6\sqrt{3}. $$

For an equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle, the circumradius is

$$ R=\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}. $$

Hence,

$$ r=\frac{6\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}}=6. $$

Therefore,

$$ r^2=36. $$

So,

$$ g^2+f^2-25=36 $$

which gives

$$ g^2+f^2=61. $$

Now solve the system

$$ -2g+f=4 $$

and

$$ g^2+f^2=61. $$

From the first equation,

$$ f=4+2g. $$

Substitute into the second:

$$ g^2+(4+2g)^2=61 $$

$$ g^2+16+16g+4g^2=61 $$

$$ 5g^2+16g-45=0. $$

Solve:

$$ 5g^2+16g-45=0. $$

Discriminant:

$$ \Delta=16^2-4\cdot 5\cdot(-45)=256+900=1156=34^2. $$

Thus,

$$ g=\frac{-16\pm 34}{10}. $$

So,

$$ g=\frac{18}{10}=\frac95 \quad \text{or} \quad g=\frac{-50}{10}=-5. $$

Now centre is $(-g,-f)$ and must be in the first quadrant.

If $g=\frac95$, then

$$ f=4+2\cdot \frac95=\frac{38}{5}, $$

so centre is

$$ \left(-\frac95,-\frac{38}{5}\right), $$

not in first quadrant.

Hence reject.

If $g=-5$, then

$$ f=4+2(-5)=-6. $$

So centre is

$$ (5,6), $$

which is in the first quadrant.

Thus the circle is

$$ x^2+y^2-10x-12y+25=0, $$

with centre $(5,6)$ and radius $6$.

Now we need the chord cut by the line

$$ x=1. $$

Distance of the line $x=1$ from the centre $(5,6)$ is

$$ |5-1|=4. $$

If a chord is at distance $d$ from the centre in a circle of radius $r$, then chord length is

$$ 2\sqrt{r^2-d^2}. $$

So here chord length is

$$ 2\sqrt{6^2-4^2}=2\sqrt{36-16}=2\sqrt{20}=4\sqrt{5}. $$

Therefore, the square of the chord length is

$$ (4\sqrt{5})^2=80. $$

Hence, the required answer is

$$ \boxed{80}. $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The centre of the circle passing through (0, 0) and (1, 0) and touching the circle $${x^2}\, + \,{y^2} = 9$$ is :
questions.solution:
Let the required circle be

$${x^2} + {y^2} + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0$$

Since it passes through $$\left( {0,0} \right)$$ and $$\left( {1,0} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow c = 0$$ and $$g = - {1 \over 2}$$

Points $$\left( {0,0} \right)$$ and $$\left( {1,0} \right)$$ lie inside the circle $${x^2} + {y^2} = 9,$$

so two circles touch internally

$$ \Rightarrow c{}_1{c_2} = {r_1} - {r_2}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\sqrt {{g^2} + {f^2}} = 3 - \sqrt {{g^2} + {f^2}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \sqrt {{g^2} + {f^2}} = {3 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {f^2} = {9 \over 4} - {1 \over 4} = 2$$

$$\therefore$$ $$f = \pm \sqrt 2 .$$

Hence, the centers of required circle are

$$\left( {{1 \over 2}.\sqrt 2 } \right)$$ or $$\left( {{1 \over 2}, - \sqrt 2 } \right)$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
A circle touches the y-axis at the point (0, 4)
and passes through the point (2, 0). Which of
the following lines is not a tangent to this circle?
questions.solution:
Equation of family of circle touching y-axis at

(0, 4) is given by (x – 0)2 + (y – 4)2 + $$\lambda $$x = 0.

$$ \because $$ It passes through (2, 0)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\lambda $$ = –10.

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Required circle is (x – 0)2 + (y – 4)2 – 10x = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ x2 + y2 – 10x – 8y + 16 = 0

$$ \therefore $$ center of circle (5, 4) and radius = 5

By checking all options you can see 4x + 3y – 8 = 0 is not a tangent to the circle.

As distance of 4x + 3y – 8 = 0 from (5, 4)

= $$\left| {{{24} \over 5}} \right|$$ $$ \ne $$ radius

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
If one of the diameters of the circle x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> - 2x - 6y + 6 = 0 is a chord of another circle 'C',
whose center is at (2, 1), then its radius is ________.
questions.solution:
Circle x2 + y2 - 2x - 6y + 6 = 0 has centre O1(1, 3) and radius r = 2.

Let centre O2 (2, 1) of required circle and its radius being r.


Distance between (1, 3) and (2, 1) is $$\sqrt 5 $$

$$ \therefore $$ $${\left( {\sqrt 5 } \right)^2} + {(2)^2} = {r^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow r = 3$$
